title: Use the trig angle addition identities
description: Find the trig values of sums of angles whose individual trig values are known.
topic_path: Use the trig angle addition identities

hints: We don't know what $\angle ABC$ is exactly, so we can't directly evaluate this function. We do know what $\sin(\angle ABC)$ is, though.

To simplify this formula to something we can use, we try the sine addition/subtraction identity: $\sin(x \pm y) = \sin x \cdot \cos y \pm \cos x \cdot \sin y$

 In this case, we have

 $\qquad \sin(\angle ABC - 60^{\circ}) =$ 
 
 $\qquad\qquad \sin(\angle ABC) \cdot \cos(60^{\circ}) - \cos(\angle ABC) \cdot \sin(60^{\circ})$

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \sin(\angle ABC) = \dfrac{Opposite}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{AC} {AB} = \dfrac{4}{5}$

 $\qquad \cos(60^{\circ}) = \dfrac{1}{2}$

 $\qquad \cos(\angle ABC) = \dfrac{Adjacent}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{BC} {AB} = \dfrac{3}{5}$

 $\qquad \sin(60^{\circ}) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad \dfrac{4}{5} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{3}{5} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \dfrac{2}{5}-\dfrac{3}{10}\sqrt{3} $
We don't know what $\angle BAC$ is exactly, so we can't directly evaluate this function. We do know what $\sin(\angle BAC)$ is, though.

To simplify this formula to something we can use, we try the sine addition/subtraction identity: $\sin(x \pm y) = \sin x \cdot \cos y \pm \cos x \cdot \sin y$

 In this case, we have

 $\qquad \sin(\angle BAC - 60^{\circ}) =$ 
 
 $\qquad\qquad \sin(\angle BAC) \cdot \cos(60^{\circ}) - \cos(\angle BAC) \cdot \sin(60^{\circ})$

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \sin(\angle BAC) = \dfrac{Opposite}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{BC} {AB} = \dfrac{5}{13}$

 $\qquad \cos(60^{\circ}) = \dfrac{1}{2}$

 $\qquad \cos(\angle BAC) = \dfrac{Adjacent}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{AC} {AB} = \dfrac{12}{13}$

 $\qquad \sin(60^{\circ}) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad \dfrac{5}{13} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{12}{13} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \dfrac{5}{26}-\dfrac{6}{13}\sqrt{3} $
We don't know what $\angle ABC$ is exactly, so we can't directly evaluate this function. We do know what $\sin(\angle ABC)$ is, though.

We don't know what $\angle ABC$ is exactly, so we can't directly evaluate this function. We do know what $\cos(\angle ABC)$ is, though.

To simplify this formula to something we can use, we try the cosine addition/subtraction identity: $\cos(x \pm y) = \cos x \cdot \cos y \mp \sin x \cdot \sin y$

 In this case, we have

 $\qquad \cos(\angle ABC - 60^{\circ}) =$
 
 $\qquad\qquad \cos(\angle ABC) \cdot \cos(60^{\circ}) + \sin(\angle ABC) \cdot \sin(60^{\circ})$

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \cos(\angle ABC) = \dfrac{Adjacent}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{BC} {AB} = \dfrac{8}{10}$

 $\qquad \cos(60^{\circ}) = \dfrac{1}{2}$

 $\qquad \sin(\angle ABC) = \dfrac{Opposite}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{AC} {AB} = \dfrac{6}{10}$

 $\qquad \sin(60^{\circ}) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad \dfrac{8}{10} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{6}{10} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \dfrac{2}{5}+\dfrac{3}{10}\sqrt{3} $
We don't know what $\angle BAC$ is exactly, so we can't compute $\sin(2 \cdot \angle BAC$) to directly evaluate this function. We do know what $\sin(\angle BAC)$ and $\cos(\angle BAC)$ are, though.

To simplify this formula to something we can use, we try the sine double-angle identity: $\sin(2x) = 2 \sin (x) \cos (x)$

 In this case, we have

 $\qquad \sin(2 \cdot \angle BAC) = 2 \sin(\angle BAC) \cos(\angle BAC) $

 (To cut down on the number of identities you have to memorize, you can derive this quickly from the angle addition identity for sine) [[☃ explanation 1]]

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \sin(\angle BAC) = \dfrac{Opposite}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{BC} {AB} = \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{10}}$

 $\qquad \cos(\angle BAC) = \dfrac{Adjacent}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{AC} {AB} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{10}}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad 2 \cdot \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{10}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{10}} = \dfrac{3}{5} $
We don't know what $\angle BAC$ is exactly, so we can't compute $\sin(2 \cdot \angle BAC$) to directly evaluate this function. We do know what $\sin(\angle BAC)$ and $\cos(\angle BAC)$ are, though.

 $\qquad \sin(2 \cdot \angle BAC) = 2 \sin(\angle BAC) \cos(\angle BAC) $

 (To cut down on the number of identities you have to memorize, you can derive this quickly from the angle addition identity for sine) [[☃ explanation 1]]

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \sin(\angle BAC) = \dfrac{Opposite}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{BC} {AB} = \dfrac{4}{2\sqrt{5}}$

 $\qquad \cos(\angle BAC) = \dfrac{Adjacent}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{AC} {AB} = \dfrac{2}{2\sqrt{5}}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad 2 \cdot \dfrac{4}{2\sqrt{5}} \cdot \dfrac{2}{2\sqrt{5}} = \dfrac{4}{5} $
We don't know what $\angle ABC$ is exactly, so we can't compute $\sin(2 \cdot \angle ABC$) to directly evaluate this function. We do know what $\sin(\angle ABC)$ and $\cos(\angle ABC)$ are, though.

To simplify this formula to something we can use, we try the sine double-angle identity: $\sin(2x) = 2 \sin (x) \cos (x)$

 In this case, we have

 $\qquad \sin(2 \cdot \angle ABC) = 2 \sin(\angle ABC) \cos(\angle ABC) $

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \sin(\angle ABC) = \dfrac{Opposite}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{AC} {AB} = \dfrac{4}{5}$

 $\qquad \cos(\angle ABC) = \dfrac{Adjacent}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{BC} {AB} = \dfrac{3}{5}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad 2 \cdot \dfrac{4}{5} \cdot \dfrac{3}{5} = \dfrac{24}{25} $

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \sin(\angle BAC) = \dfrac{Opposite}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{BC} {AB} = \dfrac{4}{5}$

 $\qquad \cos(\angle BAC) = \dfrac{Adjacent}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{AC} {AB} = \dfrac{3}{5}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad 2 \cdot \dfrac{4}{5} \cdot \dfrac{3}{5} = \dfrac{24}{25} $
We don't know what $\angle ABC$ is exactly, so we can't compute $\sin(2 \cdot \angle ABC$) to directly evaluate this function. We do know what $\sin(\angle ABC)$ and $\cos(\angle ABC)$ are, though.

To simplify this formula to something we can use, we try the sine double-angle identity: $\sin(2x) = 2 \sin (x) \cos (x)$

 In this case, we have

 $\qquad \sin(2 \cdot \angle ABC) = 2 \sin(\angle ABC) \cos(\angle ABC) $

 (To cut down on the number of identities you have to memorize, you can derive this quickly from the angle addition identity for sine) [[☃ explanation 1]]

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \sin(\angle ABC) = \dfrac{Opposite}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{AC} {AB} = \dfrac{8}{10}$

 $\qquad \cos(\angle ABC) = \dfrac{Adjacent}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{BC} {AB} = \dfrac{6}{10}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad 2 \cdot \dfrac{8}{10} \cdot \dfrac{6}{10} = \dfrac{24}{25} $
We don't know what $\angle ABC$ is exactly, so we can't compute $\sin(2 \cdot \angle ABC$) to directly evaluate this function. We do know what $\sin(\angle ABC)$ and $\cos(\angle ABC)$ are, though.

To simplify this formula to something we can use, we try the sine double-angle identity: $\sin(2x) = 2 \sin (x) \cos (x)$

 In this case, we have

 $\qquad \sin(2 \cdot \angle ABC) = 2 \sin(\angle ABC) \cos(\angle ABC) $

 (To cut down on the number of identities you have to memorize, you can derive this quickly from the angle addition identity for sine)  [[☃ explanation 1]]

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \sin(\angle ABC) = \dfrac{Opposite}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{AC} {AB} = \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{13}}$

 $\qquad \cos(\angle ABC) = \dfrac{Adjacent}{Hypotenuse} = \dfrac{BC} {AB} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad 2 \cdot \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{13}} \cdot \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}} = \dfrac{12}{13} $
We don't know what $\angle ABC$ is exactly, so we can't compute $\cos(2 \cdot \angle ABC$) to directly evaluate this function. We do know what $\sin(\angle ABC)$ and $\cos(\angle ABC)$ are, though.

To simplify this formula to something we can use, we try the cosine double-angle identity: $\cos(2x) = \cos^2 (x) - \sin^2 (x)$

 In this case, we have

 $\qquad \cos(2 \cdot \angle ABC) = \cos^2(\angle ABC) - \sin^2(\angle ABC) $

 (To cut down on the number of identities you have to memorize, you can derive this quickly from the angle addition identity for cosine)  [[☃ explanation 1]]

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \cos^2(\angle ABC) = \left(\dfrac{Adj}{Hyp}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{BC} {AB}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{4} {2\sqrt{5}}\right)^2 = \dfrac{16}{20}$

 $\qquad \sin^2(\angle ABC) = \left(\dfrac{Opp}{Hyp}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{AC} {AB}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{2} {2\sqrt{5}}\right)^2 = \dfrac{4}{20}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad \dfrac{16}{20} - \dfrac{4}{20} = \dfrac{3}{5} $
We don't know what $\angle ABC$ is exactly, so we can't compute $\cos(2 \cdot \angle ABC$) to directly evaluate this function. We do know what $\sin(\angle ABC)$ and $\cos(\angle ABC)$ are, though.

To simplify this formula to something we can use, we try the cosine double-angle identity: $\cos(2x) = \cos^2 (x) - \sin^2 (x)$

 In this case, we have

 $\qquad \cos(2 \cdot \angle ABC) = \cos^2(\angle ABC) - \sin^2(\angle ABC) $

 (To cut down on the number of identities you have to memorize, you can derive this quickly from the angle addition identity for cosine) [[☃ explanation 1]]

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \cos^2(\angle ABC) = \left(\dfrac{Adj}{Hyp}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{BC} {AB}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{3} {5}\right)^2 = \dfrac{9}{25}$

 $\qquad \sin^2(\angle ABC) = \left(\dfrac{Opp}{Hyp}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{AC} {AB}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{4} {5}\right)^2 = \dfrac{16}{25}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad \dfrac{9}{25} - \dfrac{16}{25} = -\dfrac{7}{25} $
We don't know what $\angle BAC$ is exactly, so we can't compute $\cos(2 \cdot \angle BAC$) to directly evaluate this function. We do know what $\sin(\angle BAC)$ and $\cos(\angle BAC)$ are, though.

To simplify this formula to something we can use, we try the cosine double-angle identity: $\cos(2x) = \cos^2 (x) - \sin^2 (x)$

 In this case, we have

 $\qquad \cos(2 \cdot \angle BAC) = \cos^2(\angle BAC) - \sin^2(\angle BAC) $

 (To cut down on the number of identities you have to memorize, you can derive this quickly from the angle addition identity for cosine) [[☃ explanation 1]]

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \cos^2(\angle BAC) = \left(\dfrac{Adj}{Hyp}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{AC} {AB}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{8} {10}\right)^2 = \dfrac{64}{100}$

 $\qquad \sin^2(\angle BAC) = \left(\dfrac{Opp}{Hyp}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{BC} {AB}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{6} {10}\right)^2 = \dfrac{36}{100}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad \dfrac{64}{100} - \dfrac{36}{100} = \dfrac{7}{25} $
We don't know what $\angle BAC$ is exactly, so we can't compute $\cos(2 \cdot \angle BAC$) to directly evaluate this function. We do know what $\sin(\angle BAC)$ and $\cos(\angle BAC)$ are, though.

To simplify this formula to something we can use, we try the cosine double-angle identity: $\cos(2x) = \cos^2 (x) - \sin^2 (x)$

 In this case, we have

 $\qquad \cos(2 \cdot \angle BAC) = \cos^2(\angle BAC) - \sin^2(\angle BAC) $

 (To cut down on the number of identities you have to memorize, you can derive this quickly from the angle addition identity for cosine) [[☃ explanation 1]]

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \cos^2(\angle BAC) = \left(\dfrac{Adj}{Hyp}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{AC} {AB}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{1} {\sqrt{10}}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{10}$

 $\qquad \sin^2(\angle BAC) = \left(\dfrac{Opp}{Hyp}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{BC} {AB}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{3} {\sqrt{10}}\right)^2 = \dfrac{9}{10}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad \dfrac{1}{10} - \dfrac{9}{10} = -\dfrac{4}{5} $

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \sin^2(\angle ABC) = \left(\dfrac{Opp}{Hyp}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{AC} {AB}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{3} {\sqrt{13}}\right)^2 = \dfrac{9}{13}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad \dfrac{4}{13} - \dfrac{9}{13} = -\dfrac{5}{13} $

Now we just need to evaluate each term.

 $\qquad \cos^2(\angle BAC) = \left(\dfrac{Adj}{Hyp}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{AC} {AB}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{3} {5}\right)^2 = \dfrac{9}{25}$

 $\qquad \sin^2(\angle BAC) = \left(\dfrac{Opp}{Hyp}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{BC} {AB}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{4} {5}\right)^2 = \dfrac{16}{25}$

Putting it together, we get

 $\qquad \dfrac{9}{25} - \dfrac{16}{25} = -\dfrac{7}{25} $
